1: ve'eleh hamishpatim asher tasim lifneihem (wĕʾēlleh hammiŝpāṭîm ʾăšer tāśîm lipnêhem): 'And these are the ordinances/judgments you shall set before them.' Mishpatim (H4941, judgments/ordinances): from shafat (H8199, judge/govern) — these are the legal applications of the Decalogue's principles. The Book of the Covenant (Exod 21-23) is the first legal code given to Israel, covering concrete cases. Tasim lifneihem (place before them): the same verb used for placing food before guests and for presenting arguments in court — the laws are set out for the community's engagement, not merely decreed from above.
2: ki tiqneh eved ivri shesh shanim ya'avod uvashvii yetse laChofshi chinam: 'When you buy a Hebrew slave, he shall serve six years, and in the seventh he shall go free, for nothing.' The debt-slavery law applies the Sabbath principle to servitude: six years of service; the seventh is freedom. The same seven-year structure (shemitah, the sabbatical year) governs land use (Leviticus 25:1-7) and debt release (Deuteronomy 15:1-11). The Sabbath pattern extends across all dimensions of Israelite life: work and rest, land and fallow, slavery and freedom.
3: im-begapo yavo begapo yetse im-ba'al-isha hu veyatsa ishto imo: 'If he came in single, he shall go out single; if he came in married, his wife shall go out with him.' The law preserves the integrity of pre-existing family bonds: the master who receives a slave with a wife cannot dissolve the marriage. The slave's domestic relationships are not extinguished by his servitude — a fundamental acknowledgment of the slave's personhood beyond his economic function.
4: im-adonav yiten lo isha veyalda lo banim o vanot ha'isha veyeladeha tihyeh la'adoneiha vehu yetse vegapo: 'If his master gives him a wife and she bears him sons or daughters, the wife and her children shall be her master's, and he shall go out alone.' The hard case: a master-arranged marriage produces children who belong to the master. The tension between the slave's freedom and his family creates the scenario of Exodus 21:5-6 — the voluntary permanent slavery. The law acknowledges the painful cost of freedom: some bonds exceed the value of liberty.
5: ve'im-amor yomar ha'eved ahavti et-adoni et-ishti ve'et-banai lo etse chofshi: 'But if the slave plainly says: I love my master, my wife, and my children; I will not go free.' Ahavareti et-adoni (I love my master): love as the motive for voluntary permanent slavery — the unusual circumstance where a freed person chooses permanent servitude out of love. Psalms 40:6-8 (LXX: a body you prepared for me; in the scroll it is written of me — I delight to do your will, O God) applies this exact law typologically to Christ: the one who could go free (pre-incarnate) chose permanent embodied servitude out of love. Hebrews 10:5-7 cites the Psalm as the Servant's own words.
6: vehigio adonav el-ha'Elohim vehigio el-hadelet o el-hamezuzah vertza adonav et-ozno bamartze va'avado le'olam: 'His master shall bring him to God, and he shall bring him to the door or doorpost. His master shall pierce his ear with an awl, and he shall be his slave forever.' The ear pierced at the doorpost: the doorpost is the location of the Passover blood (Exodus 12:7, 22-23) and of the mezuzah (Deuteronomy 6:9). The ear attached to the doorpost makes the body physically connected to the house — the voluntary permanent slave is bound by his own body to the place of covenant. Le'olam (forever/to the age): permanent belonging through love-chosen submission.
7: vechi-yimkor ish et-bito le'amah lo tetse ketset ha'avadim: 'When a man sells his daughter as a maidservant, she shall not go out as male servants do.' The special provisions for female debt-slaves acknowledge the additional vulnerability of women in servitude — her exit conditions are different from male slaves. The law is not a license for the sale of daughters but a regulation that limits exploitation within a practice already embedded in the ancient economy.
8: im-ra'ah be'einei adoneiha asher-lo yo'adah vefadah le'am nachri lo-yimshol limkoraha bibegdo-vah: 'If she does not please her master who designated her for himself, he shall let her be redeemed. He has no right to sell her to a foreign people, since he has dealt faithlessly with her.' The protection against exploitation: if a master fails to fulfill his obligations toward a designated slave-wife, he loses the right to sell her. Bigdo vah (dealing faithlessly with her) — the same root as beged (treachery/garment-deceit) in covenant language. The slave has rights; the master cannot simply dispose of her.
9: ve'im-livno yi'adennah kemishpat habanot ya'aseh-lah: 'If he designates her for his son, he shall treat her as a daughter.' The full daughter-status for the slave-wife given to the master's son — she is integrated into the family with full family rights. The law consistently moves toward humanization and integration rather than mere regulation of exploitation.
10: im-acheret yiqach-lo she'erah kesutah ve'onatah lo yigra: 'If he takes another wife to himself, he shall not diminish her food, her clothing, or her marital rights.' The three inviolable rights of the first wife when a second is taken: food, clothing, and conjugal rights (onah, H5772). Even within a polygynous arrangement, the law protects the first wife's basic claims. Marital obligation is a legal minimum that cannot be reduced by preference for a newer wife.
11: ve'im-shlosh-eleh lo ya'aseh-lah veyatsa chinam ein kasef: 'If he does not do these three things for her, she shall go out for nothing, without payment of money.' The slave-wife's exit right when the master defaults on his obligations: she is free, without cost to her. The law creates accountability for the master who makes covenantal commitments — failure to fulfill the three obligations transfers freedom back to the woman. Covenant obligations are enforceable even for the powerless.
12: makeh ish vamet mot yumat: 'Whoever strikes a man so that he dies shall be put to death.' The homicide law establishes capital punishment for intentional killing — grounded in Genesis 9:6 (whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed). The death-penalty for murder is not a societal deterrent but a covenantal response to the violation of the image of God in the victim: the killer has assaulted the image of YHWH and must answer with his own life.
13: va'asher lo tzadah veha'Elohim inah leyado vesamti lecha makom asher yanus shamah: 'But if he did not lie in wait, God delivered him into his hand — I will appoint you a place where he may flee.' The unintentional manslayer law introduces the concept of divine causation in accidental death: ha'Elohim inah leyado (God caused it to come into his hand). Accidental death occurs within divine providence, not outside it. The cities of refuge (Numbers 35:6-34) implement this provision — a place of safety for those who kill without intent. The distinction between intent and accident is foundational to biblical jurisprudence.
14: ve'chi-yazid ish al-re'ehu lehargo vetormah mizbechi tikachenu lamut: 'But if a man willfully attacks another to kill him by cunning, you shall take him from my altar that he may die.' Betormah (with treachery/cunning): the premeditated murder that uses deception. Even the altar provides no sanctuary for the deliberate murderer — YHWH's house is not a loophole for covenant-breakers. The contrast with the accidental manslayer (who finds asylum) is sharp: motive determines the application of the law.
15: umakeh aviv ve'imo mot yumat: 'Whoever strikes his father or mother shall be put to death.' The capital extension of the fifth commandment's honor-principle: physical assault on parents is the extreme violation of the honor-commandment, treated as seriously as murder. The family is the foundational covenant unit; its destruction from within through violence against parents is equivalent to the destruction of the covenant community itself.
16: veganev ish umechrahu venimatze veyado mot yumat: 'Whoever steals a man and sells him, if he is found in his possession, shall be put to death.' The kidnapping-for-slavery law: the selling of a free person into slavery is a capital offense — the theft of a person, the most fundamental of thefts. Paul cites this law in 1 Timothy 1:10 (enslavers/kidnappers as lawbreakers). The prohibition on person-theft places the biblical tradition in fundamental opposition to chattel slavery as a system.
17: umeqalel aviv ve'imo mot yumat: 'Whoever curses his father or mother shall be put to death.' Qalel (H7043, curse/treat with contempt/make light): the opposite of kabed (honor). To curse parents is to treat them as worthless — the maximum violation of the fifth commandment. The law creates a symmetry: the death-penalty for striking (Exodus 21:15) and for cursing (Exodus 21:17) parents brackets all forms of parental assault. Jesus cites this law in Mark 7:10 to expose the Pharisees' Corban loophole as a violation of the commandment.
18: vechi-yarivu anashim vehikah ish et-re'ehu be'even o ve'egrof velo yamut venafal lemishkav: 'When men quarrel and one strikes the other with a stone or with his fist and the man does not die but takes to his bed.' The personal injury law distinguishes between assault that kills and assault that incapacitates. The graduated liability structure — full compensation for lost time plus medical care (Exodus 21:19) but not capital punishment — shows that the law tracks actual harm rather than applying blanket penalties.
19: im-yaqum vehithalek bachutz al-mishantoh veniqah hamakeh raq shivto yiten virappo yerap'e: 'If the victim rises and walks outdoors with his staff, the one who struck him shall be clear; only he shall pay for the loss of his time and shall have him thoroughly healed.' Verapo yerap'e (shall thoroughly heal): infinitive absolute — he shall see to complete healing. The assailant bears both the economic loss (lost wages) and the full medical cost. The double responsibility of economic and medical restitution is the legal embodiment of neighbor-love: the one who caused harm must bear its full cost.
20: vechi-yakeh ish et-avdo o et-amato baShevet vamet tachat yado naqom yinaqem: 'When a man strikes his slave or female slave with a rod and the slave dies under his hand, he shall be avenged.' The master's capital liability for killing a slave: naqom yinaqem (he shall be avenged/punished). The precise punishment is debated, but the principle is clear — the slave's life has legal standing. The slave is not simply the master's property to kill at will; the slave's life creates covenantal accountability for the master.
21: ach im-yom o yomayim ya'amod lo yinaqem ki caspo hu: 'But if the slave survives a day or two, he is not to be avenged, for the slave is his money.' The provision for a slave who lingers after assault and then dies: the delay is taken as evidence that death was not the intent (Mesopotamian law codes contain similar provisions). The phrase ki caspo hu (for he is his money/property) is ethically uncomfortable but legally realistic: the master's economic self-interest presumably argues against murdering his own slave.
22: vechi-yinatzu anashim venagfu isha harah veyatze'u yeladeiha velo yihyeh ason: 'When men strive and strike a pregnant woman, and her children come out but there is no harm.' The case of miscarriage caused by bystander injury — establishing legal liability for injury to a pregnant woman. Velo yihyeh ason (no further harm/disaster): if the woman and child are not further harmed, a fine is imposed. The legal protection of the pregnant woman extends to the pregnancy itself.
23: ve'im ason yihyeh venatata nefesh tachat nafesh (wĕʾim-ʾāsôn yihyeh wĕnātattāh nāpeš taḥat nāpeš): 'But if there is harm, then you shall pay life for life.' Nefesh tachat nefesh (life for life): the lex talionis — the principle of proportional justice. The law is not a license for personal vengeance but a limit on it: the response must be proportional to the injury, no more. The lex talionis protects the accused from disproportionate punishment by legally capping the penalty at equivalence. Jesus's teaching on turning the other cheek (Matthew 5:38-39) is not the abolition of just legal claims but the transformation of the personal response to injury.
24: ayin tachat ayin shen tachat shen yad tachat yad regel tachat ragel: 'Eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.' The fourfold list of specific equivalences makes the proportionality principle concrete. In most ancient Near Eastern legal contexts, the lex talionis applied literally only in theory; in practice, monetary compensation was accepted. The Mishnah (Bava Qamma Exodus 8:1) interprets the law as requiring monetary compensation. The principle is justice, not physical retaliation for its own sake.
25: kevi'ah tachat kevi'ah petza tachat patza chaburah tachat chaburah: 'Burn for burn, wound for wound, stripe for stripe.' The extension of the lex talionis to injuries less than loss of limb: burns, wounds, bruises. The principle of proportional equivalence covers the full spectrum of bodily injury. The comprehensive list — from life (Exodus 21:23) to eye/tooth/hand/foot (Exodus 21:24) to burns/wounds/stripes (Exodus 21:25) — ensures that every level of harm has a proportional legal response.
26: vechi-yakeh ish et-ein avdo o et-ein amato veshichetah lachofshi yeshalachenu tachat eino: 'When a man strikes the eye of his slave, male or female, and destroys it, he shall let the slave go free because of his eye.' The eye-for-eye principle applied to slave-injury produces not physical equivalence but freedom: the master who permanently injures his slave's eye loses ownership. The lex talionis in the slave-injury context (Exodus 21:26-27) functions as a liberation principle — injury to the eye or tooth of a slave purchases freedom.
27: ve'im-shen avdo o shen amato yapil lachofshi yeshalachenu tachat shino: 'If he knocks out the tooth of his slave, male or female, he shall let the slave go free for his tooth's sake.' The tooth-for-tooth principle applied to slaves: a knocked-out tooth purchases freedom. Even the smallest permanent injury to a slave (teeth are the smallest body part specifically mentioned in the lex talionis) triggers the freedom clause. The slave's bodily integrity creates a legal claim on freedom — the most radical expression of the Israelite slave-protection laws.
28: vechi-yigach shor et-ish o et-isha vamet saqol yisaqel hashor velo ye'achel et-besaro uva'al hashor naqui: 'When an ox gores a man or woman to death, the ox shall be stoned, and its flesh shall not be eaten, but the owner of the ox shall be free of liability.' The ox-goring law: the ox is executed (stoning, the same death as for human murderers — reflecting the seriousness of life taken) but the owner bears no liability in the first instance. The law presupposes that an animal's behavior is not foreseeable without prior notice.
29: ve'im shor nagach hu mitmol shilshom vehu'ad bibe'alav velo yishmerenu vaheimit ish o isha hashor yisaqel vegam-be'alav yumat: 'But if the ox had a habit of goring in the past, and its owner has been warned but did not keep it in, and it kills a man or woman, the ox shall be stoned, and its owner also shall be put to death.' The ox with a known goring habit: the owner who receives warning and does not restrain his dangerous animal bears capital liability when the animal kills. Foreseeable harm creates culpability — the principle that establishes negligence as a legal category.
30: im-kofer yushat alav venat'a pidon nafsho kechol asher yushat alav: 'If a ransom is imposed on him, then he shall give for the redemption of his life whatever is imposed on him.' The ransom alternative to capital punishment for the negligent ox-owner: kofer (H3724, ransom/redemption price). The kofer is the substitutionary payment that redeems a forfeited life — the same root as the Day of Atonement's kippur (Lev 16) and the substitutionary animal sacrifice. The legal ransom principle illuminates the theological atonement vocabulary: a life at risk is redeemed by payment of a price.
31: o-ven yigach o-bat yigach kemishpat hazeh ye'aseh lo: 'Whether it gores a son or gores a daughter, the same rule applies to him.' The equality principle: the law applies uniformly regardless of the victim's age or gender. Legal protections are not differentiated by the social status of the victim — a daughter's life and a son's life are legally equivalent claims on the same standard of justice.
32: im-eved yigach hashor o amah kesef shloshim shaqalim yiten la'adonav vehashor yisaqel: 'If the ox gores a slave, male or female, the owner shall pay thirty shekels of silver to their master, and the ox shall be stoned.' Thirty shekels of silver: the legal value of a slave's life. This is the same amount paid to Judas for betraying Jesus (Matthew 26:15; Exodus 27:9-10) — Zechariah's price of a slave (Zechariah 11:12-13). The number connecting the slave-valuation law to the betrayal price is the gospel's most concentrated use of the lex talionis: Christ's life valued as a slave's ransom precisely so that he might be the ransom for many (Mark 10:45).
33: vechi-yiftach ish bor o ki-yichre ish bor velo yechasennu venaphal shamah shor o chamor: 'When a man opens a pit, or when a man digs a pit and does not cover it, and an ox or donkey falls into it.' The pit-hazard law: liability for the uncovered pit extends to any animal that falls in. The law requires proactive hazard management — not merely avoiding direct injury but taking responsibility for dangerous conditions one creates. The duty of care extends beyond direct action to the foreseeable consequences of one's omissions.
34: ba'al habor yeshallem kesef yashiv livnehem vehamet yihyeh-lo: 'The owner of the pit shall make restitution; he shall give money to its owner, and the dead animal shall be his.' The restitution principle: full market value owed for the lost animal, with the owner receiving the carcass. The economic calculus of damage-and-restitution establishes that the party who created the hazard bears the full cost of the harm it causes.
35: vechi-yigof shor ish et-shor re'ehu vamet umakeru et-hashor hachai vechalelu et-kaspo vesat hachama hameit yechetzun: 'When one man's ox injures another's ox and it dies, they shall sell the live ox and share its price, and the dead animal they shall also share.' The civil liability for animal-on-animal harm: the loss is shared equally between the owner of the injuring animal and the owner of the injured animal — a default risk-sharing principle when neither owner is negligent.
36: o noda ki nagach hashor mitmol shilshom velo yishmerenu be'alav shalem yeshalem shor tachat hashor vehamet yihyeh-lo: 'Or if it is known that the ox had a habit of goring in the past and its owner did not keep it in, he shall repay ox for ox, and the dead ox shall be his.' The known-danger negligence case in animal injury: full liability when prior goring established foreseeability. The law closes with the principle that prior knowledge creates full responsibility — the same principle underlying the warnings about the consequences of covenant violation that YHWH gives throughout the Torah. Knowledge of the law creates accountability under it.
